imageCaption: A door that exists in no visible spectrum stands in an impossible garden, its surface reflecting not light but possibility itself—every glitch zone they've healed, every choice they've made without planning, every moment of genuine spontaneity creating the pattern of its threshold.
text:
The door didn't exist in a way Aaliyah's eyes could fully process.

That was her first thought as they approached it. Her second thought was that her first thought was inadequate, because obviously doors existed. Doors were physical objects. They had frames and hinges and the capacity to be open or closed. But this door seemed to exist in the spaces between those states—not open, not closed, but somehow both simultaneously, as if the concept of a door had been asked to do too many things at once and had decided that existing in superposition was the only reasonable response.

"It's beautiful," Luisana said quietly. "It's terrifying and I have absolutely no idea what I'm looking at, but it's beautiful."

"That's because it's made of probability," Elijah said with the certainty of someone who had stopped being surprised by anything approximately forty-eight hours ago. "Look—you can see all the different versions of it at the same time. In that part, it's wood. In that part, it's metal. In that part, it's just the idea of a door and nothing's actually there at all."

Aaliyah forced her mind into diagram mode. This was what she did when reality decided to be unhelpful—she translated chaos into systems. And if she looked at the door not as a physical object but as a map of possibility states, she could almost see it.

"It's a probability manifold," she said slowly. "It's showing every possible configuration of a door that could theoretically exist, and they're all happening at once."

"Which means," Isla said—she'd followed them into the garden, though Aaliyah had been pretty certain she'd just said she couldn't—"that which one you choose to walk through depends entirely on what you're choosing to become."

"Oh good," Anthony said. He was still holding the pegboard under his arm. "Philosophical architectural decisions. This is fine. This is definitely fine. I once booked flights to an airport that turned out to be in the wrong country. I can handle a door made of probability."

Sully, meanwhile, had walked directly up to the door and shoved his nose through what appeared to be its surface. The dog's head vanished up to his neck, then somehow became unstuck from the plane of reality and appeared three feet to the left. He didn't seem bothered by this. He seemed, in fact, delighted. He barked that same impossible bark—not a warning, not an alert, but something else. Something like recognition.

"I think," Elijah said, "Sully's telling us the door is safe."

"Sully once tried to befriend a sentient temporal distortion," Aaliyah pointed out. "His safety assessment isn't reliable."

"True," Elijah conceded. "But also he's still here, isn't he? He didn't get erased or paradoxed or turned inside-out. If Sully went through something dangerous and survived it, maybe it means the dangerous thing wasn't actually dangerous. Maybe it means Sully is reading it right."

There was a logic to this that Aaliyah couldn't quite dismiss, no matter how much her rational brain wanted to.

"What happens if we go through?" Luisana asked. She was holding Anthony's free hand, the pegboard pressed between them like a shared secret. "What's on the other side?"

Isla was quiet for a long moment. When she finally spoke, her voice had changed—less like someone explaining the rules of reality and more like someone admitting they didn't know them either.

"I don't know," she said. "The Cascade has limits. My ability to see forward stops at threshold moments. And this is a threshold. This is the moment where you stop healing what was broken and start building what comes next. No one can see forward from here. Not me. Not ARIA-7. Not the timeline itself. This is where your family writes something the universe hasn't accounted for yet."

Anthony made a small noise—not quite a laugh, not quite a sob. It was the noise he made when he was about to do something that would definitely change things and he was choosing to do it anyway.

"Okay," he said. "Okay. We've been flying blind this whole time anyway. At least this blind is honest about being blind."

"That's not a plan," Luisana said.

"That's never been a plan," Anthony replied. "That's always been the point."

Elijah had moved closer to the door. He was studying it with the kind of focus that usually preceded either a breakthrough or an incredibly questionable decision. Given that this was Elijah, it could theoretically be both.

"I think," he said slowly, "that we don't get to choose which door we walk through. I think we walk into all of them at the same time. I think the door is asking us which probability we want to be most real about."

Aaliyah felt her brain stutter. "That's not physically possible."

"Neither is anything else we've done," Elijah said cheerfully. "Look—if the door is made of all possible versions of a door, and we walk through it, we don't get to pick one door and ignore the others. We have to walk through the probability space itself. We have to commit to all the possibilities at once and see which one solidifies when we do."

There was something in that logic that actually made sense. Not sense-sense. Not "this obeys the laws of physics" sense. But the kind of sense that operating systems had when they were fundamentally misaligned with how the universe actually worked. The kind of sense that you arrived at when spontaneity was your only real plan.

"And Sully?" Aaliyah asked.

"Sully already knows," Elijah said. "He's wagging."

Sully was, indeed, wagging. Not cautiously. Not experimentally. But the full-body wag of a dog who understood that something good was about to happen and his job was to be as enthusiastic about it as possible.

Aaliyah looked at her family. At her father holding forty-three license plates of everywhere they'd been. At her mother ready to walk into architectural impossibility because her husband was. At her brother who was reading the universe in dog barks and butterfly flights and getting it right. At her dog who was already halfway through a door that didn't exist in any normal sense.

"Together?" she asked.

"Together," Anthony confirmed.

And they stepped forward into probability itself.

The door felt like walking through every decision they'd ever made at the same time. Like stepping into the overlap of all their possible selves and finding out which one was most real.

And on the other side—

On the other side, the house changed.
